title: The ICC Has Real Flaws. Dismantling It Without an Alternative Is Not the Answer. | Council on Foreign Relations
gist: Former State Department legal adviser John Bellinger argues Secretary of State Rubio's campaign to 'dismantle' the ICC is legally overstated and likely a domestic political message rather than a response to a real threat, and warns that killing the court without an alternative accountability mechanism would remove the only venue for prosecuting mass atrocities like Russia's war crimes in Ukraine.

insights.claim: Secretary of State Marco Rubio's July 13, 2026 Wall Street Journal op-ed announced a diplomatic campaign to dismantle the ICC 'brick by brick,' using all tools available to the U.S. government alongside allies, without proposing any alternative accountability mechanism for international crimes.
insights.speaker: John B. Bellinger III
insights.so_what: Signals a major U.S. foreign-policy pivot away from even qualified support for international war-crimes accountability mechanisms, with no replacement institution proposed.
insights.locator: U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio’s July 13 op-ed

insights.claim: Bellinger argues the campaign's timing is puzzling since the ICC has taken no new action against the U.S. in more than six years, and characterizes the effort as likely 'primarily a political message intended for a domestic audience' rather than a response to an actual legal threat.
insights.speaker: John B. Bellinger III
insights.so_what: Investors and policy-watchers should treat the ICC campaign as largely symbolic/domestic-messaging rather than a driver of near-term shifts in international law enforcement.
insights.locator: it is hard not to see the secretary’s op-ed primarily as a political message intended for a domestic audience

insights.claim: The ICC's only prior U.S.-related action was a 2020 authorization to investigate U.S. personnel in Afghanistan; the next prosecutor announced in 2021 he was 'deprioritizing' that investigation to focus on Taliban war crimes instead, meaning there has been no active U.S. investigation for roughly five years.
insights.speaker: John B. Bellinger III
insights.so_what: Undercuts the stated urgency of the dismantlement campaign; the concrete U.S.-related case was effectively shelved years ago.
insights.locator: her successor as prosecutor announced in 2021

insights.claim: Because the U.S. never joined the Rome Statute, the ICC has no general jurisdiction over Americans and only claims jurisdiction over offenses committed on the territory of member states — so, contrary to Rubio's claims, it has no jurisdiction over U.S. Border Patrol agents, police, or federal prosecutors acting domestically, nor over U.S. military actions in non-member Iran, nor over U.S. strikes on narco-traffickers on the high seas in the Caribbean.
insights.speaker: John B. Bellinger III
insights.so_what: A concrete legal rebuttal showing the stated threat basis for the campaign does not hold up, useful for assessing how much real operational risk U.S. personnel actually face from the ICC.
insights.locator: it’s not clear why the ICC poses a new and “intolerable threat to U.S. sovereignty”

themes.theme: History of a Cyclical US-ICC Relationship

insights.claim: U.S. ICC policy has swung by administration: Clinton signed but never submitted the Rome Statute to the Senate; Bush's first term was hostile, withdrawing the U.S. signature and securing over 100 bilateral non-surrender agreements; Bush's second term shifted to a pragmatic 'modus vivendi,' including backing the 2005 UN Security Council referral of Sudan's Darfur genocide to the ICC.
insights.speaker: John B. Bellinger III
insights.so_what: Establishes a two-decade precedent of oscillating but ultimately pragmatic U.S. ICC engagement, against which Rubio's all-out dismantlement stance is a sharp break.
insights.locator: From Clinton to Trump: a complicated relationship

insights.claim: Then-Secretary of State Condoleezza Rice argued that cutting counterterrorism aid to ICC-member allies during the war on al-Qaeda was like 'shooting ourselves in the foot,' leading Bush to waive related restrictions — an early precedent for treating ICC hostility as counterproductive to broader U.S. security cooperation.
insights.speaker: John B. Bellinger III
insights.so_what: A historical case that punitive anti-ICC measures can backfire against unrelated U.S. security interests, a risk applicable to the current dismantlement campaign.
insights.locator: Secretary of State Rice famously stated

insights.claim: Obama expanded U.S. engagement with the ICC without joining it; Trump's first term returned to hostility, sanctioning the ICC prosecutor in June 2020 over the Afghanistan probe; Biden lifted those sanctions in April 2021 — a roughly decade-long partisan oscillation now escalating further under Rubio's current campaign.
insights.speaker: John B. Bellinger III
insights.so_what: Frames the current campaign as an escalation beyond the prior policy range (sanctions) toward full dismantlement, a materially different posture.
insights.locator: The Obama administration expanded U.S. engagement with the court

insights.claim: As a senator in 2022, Marco Rubio co-sponsored Resolution 546 with Lindsey Graham endorsing the ICC as 'an international tribunal that seeks to uphold the rule of law' and urging it to investigate Russian war crimes in Ukraine — directly at odds with his current campaign to dismantle the court.
insights.speaker: John B. Bellinger III
insights.so_what: A documented policy reversal by the sitting Secretary of State, underscoring that the current stance is political rather than grounded in a consistent legal or strategic doctrine.
insights.quote: an international tribunal that seeks to uphold the rule of law
insights.locator: then-Senator Rubio joined Senator Graham in sponsoring Resolution 546

insights.claim: Members of both parties in Congress have opposed ICC jurisdictional claims over the U.S. and Israel (neither has ratified the Rome Statute) while simultaneously supporting ICC investigations of figures like Joseph Kony and the Lord's Resistance Army and of Russian leaders — showing bipartisan support for the court's core mission is compatible with bipartisan concern about jurisdictional overreach onto non-members.
insights.speaker: John B. Bellinger III
insights.so_what: Suggests a full dismantlement push may lack durable bipartisan congressional backing compared to narrower jurisdictional carve-outs for the U.S. and Israel.
insights.locator: members of both parties in Congress have strongly opposed the ICC’s claimed jurisdiction

themes.theme: The Case Against Dismantling Without an Alternative

insights.claim: A 2020 independent expert report documented real, 'possibly irreconcilable' flaws in the ICC, but Bellinger's core prescription is that the U.S. should not try to kill the court — which still provides the only venue for prosecuting mass atrocities in places like Libya, the Philippines, Sudan, and Ukraine — without first proposing a credible alternative accountability mechanism, particularly for Russian war crimes.
insights.speaker: John B. Bellinger III
insights.so_what: The central actionable policy recommendation: watch whether the administration pairs its ICC campaign with any alternative accountability proposal, which would determine whether war-crimes accountability for Russia/Ukraine has any remaining venue.
insights.locator: Before dismantling the ICC, offer an alternative

insights.claim: Bellinger notes that a 'thoughtful op-ed discussing U.S. policy to end the war with Iran or the Russia-Ukraine war would have been welcome and more timely,' implying the administration's foreign-policy communications focus on the ICC diverts attention from larger, live conflicts.
insights.speaker: John B. Bellinger III
insights.so_what: A signal that the administration's stated foreign-policy priorities (ICC) may be misaligned with the more market-relevant live conflicts (Iran, Ukraine) that actually need resolution.
insights.quote: A thoughtful op-ed discussing U.S. policy to end the war with Iran or the Russia-Ukraine war would have been welcome and more timely.
insights.locator: The threat posed by the ICC is also an odd subject
